the main objective here is to identify the fundamentals of that which generated this phenomenon. we need to understand: where is the problem situated? is it several problems or one problem? what is going on behind it?

we need to design a test that puts to the test actually the process but also tries to understand if the tool is working properly and is achieving the goals. are our assumptions about the product's selling points correct? after that we run the test and we figure a course correction for what we understand is failing and is changing.
2: yeah the ux problem is centered around usability, around cognitive load, around accessibility, around user friction. for example users can't understand how to customize the product. the business problem is about customer acquisition cost, customer monthly churn percentage, conversion rate, etc.
3:
we had engineers interested in using the cli rather than any ui. obviously they can now use a genic cli so they don't need to even visit a ui because they can ask the ilm what's going on, what kind of information they need, what format they needed, etc.

we had this amazing infrastructure that was bigger and bigger. we identified that people were accessing the architecture more often than at the beginning because the architecture was growing. when we have one component or two components or five components, let's say two uis, two frontend servers running in different ports, then two or three databases, then more components like databases, backend services, and more infrastructure like infrastructure as code service and security as a service, we end up growing a lot of our infrastructure. everybody was building their own tools but nobody understood what to look at if they didn't know exactly what their environment was looking like.

we created a react flow-based graphic. when you visit that graphic, we have all the different nodes with descriptions and some information. you could open it and play around with the service. you could fork it into your own project and use it at least as the basis of the available github repo because a lot of it was confidential and a lot of it was client-based. we had almost a sandbox for everything and a running sandbox. using this, people were allowed to discover a lot of things that existed inside our ecosystem and that was amazing because we didn't need everybody to run the same sandbox, the same backend service, because everything was actually running. they look at the interface, look at the graphic. they identify what was going on behind the graphic and they could do two things:
1. identify all the modules that they already knew existed, the new services, the new tools, the new mcp servers, etc.
2. look at the issues inside those because some of those were serving actual users inside the company and some of them were just for personal use for example a product team so they were allowed to understand the status of everything.

5: i think that the product that lives in the intersection between what a user wants, what a stakeholder wants, and what is technologically possible is going to be successful
6: mvp is a convention that we take into consideration, taking the variables that we need to adjust. we take time into consideration and a measurable amount of value that we will deliver at a certain point in time. we are able to dissect this deliverable and organize the team too so we can tackle everything around the product. so obviously it's like a minimal viable product right? we try to maximize value and then keep the effort controlled.
7:
yeah what is non-mvp? it's a really complete set of features that at a certain point in time we are not able to measure the impact. we assume or we have certain information that those features are not delivering the same value as the features and the actual core of the product we are delivering so we need to be pretty cautious about this.

in terms of things that are obvious for example:
- super polished aesthetics
- a ui that is glamorous and heavy in the web browser
- a lot of things that are obviously not part of the mvp


also things that could live in the limits of being a feature that deserves to be in an mvp and a feature that is not going to be deserving to be in an mvp. how we can decide that is the expertise of the subject matter expert. what product are we building and our stakeholders will be able to help us to identify that. it depends on the product. we will be able to understand what we are doing in terms of let's say:
- uber: we are letting people contact, ask for a ride, pay for the ride, and then go wherever they are going to


rather than a lot of features like:
- sharing your location with friends
- splitting the bill
- recording this


so it's a lot of tools that are around the most important core value.
8:
we need to measure the value of the features and try to be prescriptive so we need to research some sort of a score that we will assign to each feature. we would say what are all the metrics that we're going to expect from the features. for example if we are asking for a feature that allows users to log in faster so instead of using user email they're using google, what are the metrics? what are they? use the actual user experience improvement.

after analyzing all the features with all the metrics, we make sure that we try to come up with some sort of a system. we brainstorm a system to identify which are the priorities. when we are prioritizing, we need to make sure that we evaluate the effort from the engineering front and then we pick what we want to build with a stakeholder.

11: product discovery is when we test what we know about everything that we are trying to build. we have a lot of assumptions, a lot of content, and a lot of expertise in the area ideally. we identify pain points and then we propose solutions. what we are doing is trying to understand if those solutions that we propose are aligned with the pain points that we identify.

yeah so i usually start with the kick-off session when i want to raise my voice to identify the problem definition. that has to be in all people's minds because we need to be aligned at the beginning on the one thing that we know is a fact and that we believe is true. then we start navigating into that, going deeper and analyzing that problem, gathering all the information about it, mapping it, getting data, getting ideation, then proposing solutions with cross-functional teams or partners or everything.

then we need to prototype the solution but actually first of all i think we need to make sure that we understand the process where the process is located. we have a good context diagram then we have a good process diagram. we have a good solution for the portion of the process that we are tackling or step or portion of the process that we are tackling, which depends on the product of course. then we start prototyping, validation, usability, everything else right and then it ends up with a handoff mapping of the things that we discover and implement and design.

then i'll pick a framework to evaluate the feature in terms of what kind of feature i'll be looking for. i decide to go: if i need rise or the other models for feature request evaluation then after that i run it through the evaluation and propose some insight about that, a report on that. we make sure that it is well located in the roadmap of all the things that are existing because i have never been in a product that is empty. the work that we need to do is to understand where we have to locate it and then we start the discovery process.

it's like i feel like it's like a regression test. we don't break things that are already working so i want to have that mindset in engineering but also user-centered or oriented. i need to empathize with the user and then we make sure that these new features are going to work for them. we discovered everything about it. we run from:
- what the problem is
- solving
- what metrics are going to be improved with this feature


after this we ideate a design solution, prototyping, and hand-off design and that's it. 

in my last position we had a platform called aha so we laid out ideas from everybody including stakeholders. we run a process to identify what is the risk associated with it, the value of it. the framework that we picked then is after we decide to gather more information or we have seven or eight stages for it. after we run all the stages then we make sure that that feature is ready to go right into the backlog on jira and that's pretty much it.

i think an assumption could become a fact when you validate it with data right? the source of the data will be attached to the nature of the assumption. if the assumption is a result of an observation that we are having from analyzing data, then it will be way easier to tackle.

an assumption that is coming from the highest-paid person in the company that says "hey i think users want to perform task b not task k" could be right but we need to find data that backs up that claim. we need to use common sense. no we need to classify that assumption:
- what is the uncertainty, the level of entropy of that assumption or uncertainty?
- how uncertain is that assumption?
- what's the feasibility generally speaking when we understand that assumption, is it feasible or not feasible?
15: in my experience time and effort are the constraint. in a perfect world i will aim for 99.99% of accuracy, backing up an assumption or a hypothesis or a feature or whatever we want to build. i never reach that level of confidence but we need to agree. what is the risk associated with lowering that level of confidence and can we handle that risk or not? we can aim for 95%. the risk will be way lower than if we aim for 65% and we can calculate that based on the frame. we can pick a couple of frameworks to do that. i'm not sure which but i'm sure that there are some frameworks to tackle the accuracy of the evidence that i have for something.

17:
Obviously you're trying to extract information from users you don't know yet. At some point you had to use user interviews to correct your ideation.
You could say: "Hey, I need an app to compete with Uber because Uber is bad, in your opinion." And at some point you had to do that â either late, like when you finish spending millions of dollars implementing a new application, or right at the beginning of your project, saying: "Hey, my assumption is that Uber is bad, so you'll get a thousand interviews or a hundred interviews, or the qualitative and quantitative user testing indicators." So I would say that's the main use. It will provide you with a great picture of the market that will make you only make assumptions instead of just information.
And moving from there you can learn different things, right? Hey, I want to learn if the process is right, if this is the problem they're trying to solve. We'll learn if the UI is successive enough, reliable enough â some qualitative methodologies. Do you like it? Do you feel it's intuitive enough? Simple enough?
But also, could you ask whether your application allows a user to achieve a goal by following different paths? So you can ask: could you please accomplish this task? Then you can observe and listen to the user.
18:
ok so last project i conducted research on was the landing page for a product called trusted environment. this is a developer portal platform with several capabilities from terraforming infrastructure code all the way down to developer tooling.

in order to be effective we need to make sure that we identify what methodologies we use and how we're going to fit into the overall picture of the product. the marketing tells us the vision of the company because it was an internal initiative but also in the future trusted environment will be available to the public like bursa or netlify. we need to make sure that we will be available for internal usage, keeping in mind that these two will be kind of near future. this tool will become public.

user experience is key in this process. after choosing the right methodology we gather the information and processes using ai first and then learn about the insights of that information. we figure several things all the way down from pain point resolution to pricing, cost, operations, customer service, etc. and then we trust a roadmap, a plan for the landing page as a product. we then determine what will be the best business outcome in terms of metrics, what is how success looks like in terms of the varying metrics.
